title: GDP Requirements for Couriers and 3PLs
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A senior QP's guide to GDP courier requirements: temperature control, security, traceability, audits and quality agreements for couriers and 3PLs in transit.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury banner of a refrigerated pharmaceutical logistics scene at dawn: an insulated cold-chain shipping container and a temperature-monitoring data logger resting on a sleek brushed-steel surface, with a softly blurred refrigerated delivery vehicle and loading bay receding in the background. Cool teal and cyan tones blend into a deep navy atmosphere, lifted by a subtle green accent glowing from a monitoring display. Soft natural light rakes across the surfaces, shallow depth of field isolates the foreground container, immaculate and frost-kissed, conveying precision, security and unbroken integrity. 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: Are couriers and 3PLs required to hold a wholesale dealer's authorisation (WDA(H))?
faqs.a: A pure transport provider that only moves sealed consignments between sites generally does not need a WDA(H), as transportation itself is not wholesale dealing. However, if the courier or 3PL stores medicines, breaks bulk, or takes ownership and supplies onward, it is performing wholesale distribution and must hold the appropriate authorisation. Quality teams should confirm exactly which activities a provider performs before deciding what licensing and oversight apply.

faqs.q: Who is responsible if a temperature excursion happens during transit?
faqs.a: Under the EU GDP guidelines, the wholesale distribution authorisation holder remains responsible for the product throughout transport, even when carriage is outsourced. The courier or 3PL is contractually accountable for maintaining the agreed conditions and reporting deviations, but the disposition decision rests with your responsible person. This is why a clear quality agreement defining monitoring, notification timelines and decision rights is essential.

faqs.q: How often should we audit our couriers and logistics providers?
faqs.a: Audit frequency should be risk-based rather than fixed. Providers handling cold-chain, high-value or controlled products on complex routes typically warrant more frequent and deeper scrutiny than those moving ambient stock short distances. As a guide, an initial qualification audit followed by periodic re-audit, supported by ongoing review of excursion and performance data, allows you to escalate oversight whenever the risk profile changes.
